curriculum_alignment.subject: mathematics
curriculum_alignment.subtopic: factorising monic quadratics x^2 + bx + c using sum-and-product (integer factors)
curriculum_alignment.seeds_of_change: Factorise monic quadratics of the form x^2 + bx + c by finding integers m and n with m + n = b and m·n = c; recognise when a quadratic does not factorise over the integers; and communicate reasoning clearly using factor-pair evidence and checking by expansion. :contentReference[oaicite:0]{index=0}

verse_ality.driving_question: When can a quadratic x^2 + bx + c be written as (x + m)(x + n), and how can we prove it?

knowing_world.focus: Investigate when x^2 + bx + c factorises into (x + m)(x + n) by testing integer factor pairs of c and checking which pair sums to b, leading to the sum-and-product condition for factorisation (and spotting when no integer factorisation exists). :contentReference[oaicite:1]{index=1}
key_concepts: Sum-and-product condition: m + n = b and m·n = c
Not factorisable over the integers (no integer pair sums to b)
tools: Interactive factor-pair matcher for monic quadratics (choose/randomise b and c, test m and n, check answer)

human_story.focus: Factorising polynomials has long supported solving equations and understanding roots, from historical algebraic methods to modern applications where polynomial structure matters (e.g., modelling, signal processing, cryptography foundations). :contentReference[oaicite:2]{index=2}

expressive_self.focus: Make factorising visible and explainable by mapping factor pairs, scripting mathematical dialogue, and recording a clear reasoning narrative for one quadratic. :contentReference[oaicite:3]{index=3}
activities: Create a “factor map”: choose a quadratic and list all integer factor pairs of c, then highlight the pair that sums to b (or explain why none works).
Write a short dialogue where one student argues it factors and another tests m, n pairs using evidence.
Record a brief explanation of your reasoning for one quadratic, including a final check by expansion.

designed_world.focus: Practise factorising by selecting or randomising b and c, generating candidate factor pairs for c, and using a check feature to confirm (or rule out) integer factorisation, building speed and accuracy through repetition. :contentReference[oaicite:4]{index=4}
design_challenges: Use an interactive tool to test quadratics (including cases that do not factorise over the integers), then design your own set of examples that intentionally vary b and c to change factorability.

regulation_supports: Stand and balance: for each tested integer pair (m, n), step m times and n times (or balance on alternate feet), then reflect on how the pair combines to make the b value.

pathways.name: Final Task - 6 Monic Quadratics Challenge Set
pathways.focus: Create a set of 6 monic quadratics (some factorisable over the integers, some not) and challenge a peer to identify factor pairs and justify decisions using the sum-and-product condition.
products: A set of 6 monic quadratics x^2 + bx + c (mixed: factorisable and not factorisable over the integers)
For each quadratic: factor pair evidence (m·n = c) and sum check (m + n = b), or a clear explanation that no integer pair works
An answer key with fully factorised forms where possible and a brief check by expansion
A short reflection: which examples were hardest and why
assessment_criteria: Conceptual Understanding: Explains why m + n = b and m·n = c is necessary for (x + m)(x + n)
Fluency & Accuracy: Factorises correctly or correctly identifies non-factorisable cases over the integers
Application: Includes examples that connect to area/rectangle models or equation-solving contexts
Independence & Reflection: Reflects on difficulty, strategy, and error-checking

required: Confidence with integer factors and factor pairs
Fluency with addition and multiplication of integers (including negatives)
Basic understanding of expanding brackets to check factorisations

builds_toward: Quadratic graph features linked to roots/factors
Non-integer factorisation methods (completing the square, quadratic formula)
